review.value: Unlike some countries that regulate what can be offered to investors, US law focuses on disclosure - requiring accurate information so investors can make informed decisions. Fraud and material misstatements are actionable; legitimate but risky investments are permitted

review.key: Accuracy Obligations Apply Beyond Public Offerings
review.value: Even private offerings exempt from registration are subject to securities antifraud rules. Fundraising startups cannot deceive investors regardless of whether the offering is registered

Sarbanes-Oxley imposed significant burdens on public company executives (CEO/CFO certification) and auditors (independence requirements), dramatically raising compliance costs. The cost has led some companies to remain private longer, but has also improved financial reporting quality

SEC and DOJ pursue securities fraud aggressively. Prison sentences for significant fraud can be substantial. Executives who engage in fraud often face not just civil penalties but criminal conviction and long prison terms

class_schedule.topic: Antitrust Law: Monopoly, Collusion, Merger Review
learning_objectives: Identify the major antitrust laws: Sherman Act, Clayton Act, FTC Act
Distinguish horizontal from vertical restraints and their legal treatment
Explain merger review: HSR filings, DOJ/FTC review, market definition, competitive effects
Analyze major antitrust cases and their strategic implications
readings: Mallor et al., Business Law (18th ed.), Ch. 47 - Antitrust
Miller, Fundamentals of Business Law (11th ed.), Ch. 33
Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) Sections 1 and 2
Recent cases: Microsoft antitrust (2001), FTC v. Meta (Instagram/WhatsApp), DOJ v. Google

lecture_notes.introduction: Antitrust law prevents competitors from engaging in practices that harm competition and ultimately harm consumers through higher prices, lower quality, or reduced innovation. The US has the longest and strongest antitrust tradition in the world, starting with the Sherman Antitrust Act of 1890 passed in response to industrial trusts (like Standard Oil) that had come to dominate entire industries. The basic antitrust framework has two main prohibitions. Section 1 of the Sherman Act prohibits contracts, combinations, or conspiracies in restraint of trade - primarily targeting collusion between competitors. Examples include price-fixing, bid-rigging, market allocation, and group boycotts. Section 2 of the Sherman Act prohibits monopolization and attempts to monopolize. Having a monopoly is not itself illegal - becoming one through 'superior skill, foresight, and industry' is protected. What is illegal is using a monopoly position to engage in anticompetitive conduct or acquiring monopoly power through improper means. The Clayton Act (1914) addresses mergers, exclusive dealing, tying, and price discrimination. The FTC Act (1914) creates the Federal Trade Commission and broadly prohibits unfair methods of competition. Antitrust enforcement is handled by both the Department of Justice Antitrust Division and the FTC (with overlapping jurisdiction). Private plaintiffs can also bring antitrust suits (with treble damages and attorney fees). The enforcement environment has shifted significantly in recent years. After decades of relatively permissive merger review under Chicago School economic thinking (Bork, Posner, Easterbrook), the Biden administration has taken a more aggressive approach, pursuing cases against Google, Meta, Apple, Microsoft, and others. The pendulum has swung back toward stronger enforcement, with implications for how firms can grow and operate. This session covers the major antitrust laws, distinguishes per se illegal conduct from rule of reason analysis, addresses merger review, and examines contemporary cases.
key_concepts: Sherman Act Section 1: prohibits contracts, combinations, or conspiracies in restraint of trade; targets collusion between competitors (horizontal) or between firms at different levels of distribution (vertical)
Per se illegal: conduct so harmful to competition that it is always illegal without inquiry into effects (price-fixing, bid-rigging, market allocation between competitors, some tying arrangements)
Rule of reason: conduct evaluated based on its actual effects on competition; court weighs anticompetitive effects against procompetitive justifications; most antitrust cases follow this approach

Sherman Act Section 2: prohibits monopolization and attempts to monopolize; requires (1) possession of monopoly power in relevant market and (2) willful acquisition or maintenance through anticompetitive conduct
Relevant market definition: identifying the market in which competition is at issue; based on products and geographic area; critical in monopolization and merger cases
Clayton Act Section 7: prohibits mergers and acquisitions whose effect may be to substantially lessen competition or tend to create a monopoly
HSR filings: Hart-Scott-Rodino Antitrust Improvements Act requires premerger notification to DOJ and FTC for transactions exceeding size thresholds ($112 million in 2024); waiting period for review

Vertical restraints: agreements between firms at different levels of the supply chain (manufacturer to distributor, wholesaler to retailer); includes exclusive dealing, tying, resale price maintenance
Resale price maintenance (RPM): manufacturer specifying minimum price at which retailer can sell; historically per se illegal but now evaluated under rule of reason since Leegin (2007)
Private antitrust litigation: Section 4 of Clayton Act allows treble damages plus attorney fees for antitrust violations; creates strong incentive for private enforcement
lecture_notes.demonstration:
Analyze the Microsoft antitrust case (1998-2001) and its modern parallels. Facts: In the mid-1990s, Microsoft had monopoly power in the PC operating system market through Windows. With the emergence of the internet and Netscape Navigator web browser, Microsoft feared that web applications and the browser would erode the relevance of Windows. Microsoft responded by:

1. Integrating Internet Explorer into Windows (making it difficult to remove and bundling it with Windows so PC makers would not install Netscape).

2. Pressuring PC manufacturers (like Compaq) to not pre-install Netscape or face termination of their Windows licenses.

3. Pressuring Apple to use Internet Explorer rather than Netscape by threatening to discontinue Microsoft Office for Mac.

4. Pressuring internet service providers (like AOL) to bundle IE rather than Netscape by promising them a place in the Windows desktop.

5. Creating 'Java' incompatibility that made Java work only on Windows (potentially eliminating cross-platform Java as a threat).

DOJ filed antitrust lawsuit in 1998 arguing that Microsoft monopolized the OS market and illegally tied the browser to Windows to eliminate Netscape. Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson found Microsoft liable and ordered structural remedy (breaking Microsoft into two companies). On appeal, liability was affirmed but the breakup was reversed; case was ultimately settled in 2001 with behavioral remedies rather than structural.

Legal analysis:

Section 2 monopolization: Court found Microsoft had monopoly power in PC operating systems (over 90% market share with high barriers to entry through network effects and application developer relationships). Microsoft maintained and extended this monopoly through anticompetitive conduct described above, not just through offering a better product. The anticompetitive conduct - bundling IE with Windows, contractual restrictions on PC makers, API incompatibilities - went beyond normal competition.

Section 1 - illegal tying: bundling IE with Windows potentially violated tying prohibitions (using market power in one product to force buyers to accept another product).

Results: Settlement in 2001 required Microsoft to disclose certain APIs to competitors, not restrict PC makers from installing competitor software, not retaliate against firms that made compatible products, and comply with oversight for several years.

Strategic implications: The case did not break Microsoft up, but it changed Microsoft's behavior. Microsoft became more cautious about using Windows to leverage into adjacent markets. The case also influenced other firms' antitrust compliance.

Modern parallels: Current cases against Google (search dominance, Android restrictions), Meta (Instagram and WhatsApp acquisitions challenged), Apple (App Store policies), and Amazon (marketplace practices) raise similar issues of how dominant firms can use their market position. The Biden-era FTC (led by Lina Khan) has pursued aggressive cases against these firms, and courts will decide whether the Microsoft-era antitrust framework fits modern tech platforms.

In 2024, a federal court ruled against Google in the DOJ search antitrust case, finding that Google illegally maintained its search monopoly through exclusive contracts with Apple, Samsung, and other device makers. The remedy phase is ongoing and could potentially require structural changes. This is the most significant tech antitrust case since Microsoft.

Key lessons:

1. Monopoly power itself is not illegal, but using that power to harm competition is.

2. Even large, well-resourced firms can lose antitrust cases. Microsoft had elite lawyers and significant resources but lost both at trial and on appeal.

3. Antitrust enforcement varies over time. The Chicago School dominated from 1980-2020 with a relatively permissive approach. Current enforcement is more aggressive. Strategic decisions should consider the enforcement environment at the time of action.

4. Remedies can significantly change business practices without requiring breakup. Behavioral remedies (agreements to change conduct) are more common than structural remedies (divestitures or breakups).
setup_requirements: Current tech antitrust cases (Google, Meta, Apple, Amazon)

review.key: Antitrust Targets Harmful Conduct, Not Size
review.value: Having a large market share is not itself illegal. What is illegal is using market power to engage in conduct that harms competition and consumers. The distinction is sometimes subtle but legally important

review.key: Per Se vs Rule of Reason
review.value: Some conduct is so clearly harmful that it is per se illegal (price-fixing between competitors). Most antitrust cases are evaluated under rule of reason, which weighs anticompetitive effects against procompetitive justifications. Understanding which standard applies is crucial

Any significant acquisition must be filed with DOJ/FTC under HSR. Recent administrations have been more aggressive in challenging mergers, particularly in tech and healthcare. Firms planning acquisitions should do antitrust analysis before proceeding

The same conduct may be treated differently depending on the enforcement environment. Firms cannot simply plan based on past enforcement patterns; they must monitor changes and adapt. The current environment is more aggressive than the 1980-2020 era

class_schedule.topic: Environmental Law: NEPA, CAA, CWA, RCRA, Superfund
learning_objectives: Identify the major federal environmental statutes and their scope
Explain how environmental law is enforced through civil, criminal, and citizen suits
Analyze the business implications of environmental regulation
Evaluate emerging issues: climate change, ESG disclosure, plastic pollution
readings: Mallor et al., Business Law (18th ed.), Ch. 52 - Environmental Law
Miller, Fundamentals of Business Law (11th ed.), Ch. 36
Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, RCRA, CERCLA (overview sections)
EPA enforcement reports and major settlements

lecture_notes.introduction: Environmental law regulates activities that affect air, water, land, and wildlife. The modern environmental law era began in 1970 with the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) and the creation of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Since then, Congress has passed dozens of statutes that now impose significant compliance burdens on virtually every business. The major federal environmental statutes include: NEPA (1969, requires federal agencies to assess environmental impact of major actions); Clean Air Act (1970, amended 1977, 1990, regulates air pollution); Clean Water Act (1972, amended 1977, 1987, regulates water pollution); Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA, 1976, regulates solid and hazardous waste); Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA/Superfund, 1980, liability for hazardous waste cleanup); Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA, 1976); Endangered Species Act (1973); and many others. State environmental laws often supplement federal law with stricter requirements - California's environmental regulations are particularly strict. Environmental law enforcement includes civil penalties (often in the hundreds of millions of dollars for major violations), criminal prosecution of individuals (executives can and do go to prison for environmental crimes), injunctive relief, and citizen suits (environmental groups can sue on behalf of the public). CERCLA liability is particularly harsh - retroactive, joint and several, and covers past activities even if they were legal at the time. Major Superfund sites have cost hundreds of millions of dollars to clean up. Beyond traditional enforcement, environmental issues now intersect with securities law (ESG disclosure requirements), international trade (CBAM in EU), corporate governance (climate risk disclosure), and private civil litigation (climate liability suits against oil companies, PFAS contamination lawsuits against 3M, Dupont, Chemours). This session covers the major federal environmental laws and examines specific enforcement cases and emerging issues.
key_concepts: National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA, 1969): requires federal agencies to consider environmental impact of major federal actions; Environmental Impact Statements (EIS) document the analysis; does not require environmentally best outcome but requires consideration

Clean Air Act (CAA): regulates air pollution from stationary sources (industrial facilities) and mobile sources (vehicles); National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for criteria pollutants; State Implementation Plans; emissions trading (acid rain program)
Clean Water Act (CWA): regulates discharge of pollutants to waters of the United States; National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) permits; wetlands protection under Section 404

Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA): cradle-to-grave regulation of hazardous waste generation, transportation, treatment, storage, and disposal; Subtitle C for hazardous waste, Subtitle D for solid waste

Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA/Superfund): imposes liability for hazardous waste contamination; liability is retroactive, strict, and joint and several; Superfund sites identified on National Priorities List
Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA): regulates manufacture, import, processing, and use of chemicals; 2016 amendments (Frank R. Lautenberg Act) strengthened regulation
Endangered Species Act: protects species listed as threatened or endangered; prohibits 'taking' of listed species; critical habitat designation; consultation requirements for federal actions
Common law environmental claims: nuisance, trespass, negligence, and strict liability theories still apply to pollution cases; increasingly used against climate polluters
Environmental criminal liability: knowing violations of major environmental statutes are criminal; individual liability for corporate executives (responsible corporate officer doctrine)
ESG disclosure: growing requirements for companies to disclose environmental, social, and governance information; EU's CSRD, proposed SEC climate disclosure rules
lecture_notes.demonstration:
Analyze the BP Deepwater Horizon case as an environmental law case study. Facts: In April 2010, the Deepwater Horizon drilling rig operated by Transocean for BP exploded in the Gulf of Mexico. Eleven workers were killed. The blowout preventer failed, and oil flowed uncontrolled from the Macondo well for 87 days, releasing an estimated 4.9 million barrels of oil into the Gulf. The spill affected coastlines from Texas to Florida, killed wildlife, damaged fisheries and tourism, and caused massive economic and environmental harm.

Legal implications:

1. Clean Water Act violations: BP was subject to CWA civil penalties for the oil discharge. CWA allows penalties up to $4,300 per barrel of oil discharged (or $1,100 per barrel for negligent discharge). With 4.9 million barrels, the potential penalty was over $20 billion. BP ultimately settled for about $5.5 billion in CWA penalties alone.

2. Oil Pollution Act (OPA, 1990): Passed after Exxon Valdez, OPA imposes strict liability for oil spills up to $75 million (higher if gross negligence or willful misconduct is found). BP was found grossly negligent in the case, raising the liability cap. BP was responsible for all cleanup costs and damages.

3. Natural Resource Damages: BP was liable for damage to natural resources under multiple federal statutes. Restoration costs and compensatory damages for ecosystem harm.

4. Criminal liability: DOJ pursued criminal charges. BP pleaded guilty in 2012 to 14 felony charges including manslaughter for the 11 workers killed and obstruction of Congress. BP paid $4 billion in criminal penalties - the largest criminal penalty in US history at the time.

5. Individual liability: Criminal charges against individuals. David Rainey (former BP executive) was acquitted of obstructing Congress. Donald Vidrine and Robert Kaluza (drilling supervisors) pleaded guilty to misdemeanor charges. Not as many individual convictions as some expected.

6. Civil litigation: Private plaintiffs - fishermen, tourism businesses, property owners, states - brought thousands of lawsuits. BP set up a $20 billion Gulf Coast Claims Facility to handle private claims. Ultimate payments were higher.

7. Class action settlements: BP settled multi-district litigation with private plaintiffs for over $10 billion.

Total cost to BP: Approximately $65 billion including fines, penalties, cleanup costs, settlements, and reparations. This was among the most expensive corporate accidents in history.

Business implications:

1. Regulatory compliance is not optional for offshore drilling operators. The failures that led to the spill included decisions to skip safety procedures (well integrity testing) and proceed without proper controls - all to save time and money. The resulting cost of compliance failure was multiple orders of magnitude higher than any savings.

2. Environmental law provides broad authority for enforcement. CWA, OPA, criminal charges, private litigation, and natural resource damages all applied to the same incident. Coordinated enforcement produced substantial cumulative penalties.

3. Corporate culture and safety: Investigations found a pattern of BP corner-cutting and risk-taking. This cultural problem was the root cause, not isolated technical failures. Rebuilding safety culture is essential for high-hazard industries.

4. Criminal liability for corporate conduct: BP pleaded guilty to felonies, and the company is on probation. This creates ongoing compliance obligations and reputational damage.

5. Long-term remediation: BP spent over a decade on Gulf restoration. The full environmental and economic recovery is still not complete.

Compare: Exxon Valdez (1989, Alaska), $5 billion punitive damages (later reduced); Love Canal (1978, chemical waste); PFAS/forever chemicals cases against 3M, Dupont, Chemours; Pacific Gas & Electric Hinkley (made famous by Erin Brockovich); Volkswagen diesel scandal (environmental fraud beyond consumer protection). Each illustrates different dimensions of environmental liability.
setup_requirements: Clean Air Act and Clean Water Act overview
EPA enforcement data and major settlements

review.value: CERCLA imposes strict, joint-and-several, retroactive liability for hazardous waste cleanup. Companies can be liable for contamination caused decades ago or by former operators. Buyers of contaminated property can inherit liability. The scope is much broader than most other areas of law

Environmental statutes include criminal provisions, and prosecutors pursue them. Knowing violations can result in imprisonment for individuals. Environmental compliance is not a check-the-box exercise but a serious legal obligation with personal consequences for executives

Environmental statutes allow citizens and organizations to sue directly for violations. This creates an additional layer of enforcement beyond government agencies. Environmental groups actively monitor compliance and pursue violations

Environmental, social, and governance disclosure requirements are increasing. EU requires extensive ESG disclosure. Proposed SEC rules would require climate risk disclosure. Investors and customers increasingly demand ESG information. What was voluntary is becoming mandatory

class_schedule.topic: Corporate Social Responsibility, ESG, and Stakeholder Capitalism
learning_objectives: Compare shareholder primacy (Friedman) with stakeholder theory (Freeman)
Analyze ESG investing trends and their business implications
Evaluate corporate social responsibility programs for strategic coherence
Identify the tension between short-term shareholder returns and long-term stakeholder value
readings: Friedman, 'The Social Responsibility of Business is to Increase its Profits,' NY Times Magazine (1970)
Freeman, Strategic Management: A Stakeholder Approach (1984), selected chapters
Business Roundtable, 'Statement on the Purpose of a Corporation' (August 2019)
Porter & Kramer, 'Creating Shared Value,' Harvard Business Review (2011)

lecture_notes.introduction: The debate over what corporations owe society - and how to balance shareholder returns against other interests - is one of the most important and contested questions in business and law. For much of the 20th century, shareholder primacy was the dominant view in US corporate law and practice: corporations exist to maximize shareholder value within legal and ethical constraints, and directors have a duty to pursue this goal. Milton Friedman's 1970 New York Times Magazine article 'The Social Responsibility of Business is to Increase its Profits' became the intellectual foundation of shareholder primacy. Friedman argued that managers are agents of shareholders and have no business pursuing 'social responsibility' with shareholders' money - if shareholders want to donate to causes, they can do so themselves. Anything else is taxation without representation. The contrary view, developed by R. Edward Freeman in Strategic Management: A Stakeholder Approach (1984), argues that corporations have legitimate obligations to all stakeholders: shareholders, employees, customers, suppliers, communities, and the environment. Freeman's stakeholder theory has become the intellectual foundation of modern corporate social responsibility and ESG investing. The debate has significant practical implications. In August 2019, the Business Roundtable (representing CEOs of major US corporations) issued a statement endorsing a stakeholder view - a departure from its previous shareholder primacy stance. Larry Fink at BlackRock has consistently advocated for ESG considerations in investment decisions. Yet critics argue that stakeholder capitalism is incoherent (what happens when stakeholder interests conflict?), politically loaded (who decides which stakeholders matter?), and potentially unaccountable (managers can justify any decision by claiming stakeholder considerations). The tension has become increasingly political, with anti-ESG legislation in some states and ongoing legal disputes. This session examines the theoretical frameworks, the practical developments, and the arguments on both sides.
key_concepts: Shareholder primacy (Friedman, 1970): corporations exist to maximize shareholder value within legal and ethical constraints; managers are agents of shareholders; social responsibility efforts are unauthorized taxation of shareholders
Stakeholder theory (Freeman, 1984): corporations have obligations to all parties affected by their decisions - employees, customers, suppliers, communities, environment, not just shareholders

Business Roundtable 2019 Statement: 181 CEO signatories endorsed a stakeholder view of corporation, committing to 'deliver value to all stakeholders'; academic research shows limited behavioral change followed
Enlightened shareholder value: hybrid view arguing that maximizing long-term shareholder value requires considering stakeholder interests because they affect firm performance

Creating Shared Value (Porter & Kramer, 2011): business strategy that creates economic value in ways that also create value for society; addresses shortcomings of shareholder and stakeholder frameworks

ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance): framework for evaluating corporate performance on non-financial dimensions; used by investors for portfolio decisions and by rating agencies (MSCI, Sustainalytics)
ESG investing: investment strategies that incorporate ESG factors; assets in ESG funds exceed $30 trillion globally as of 2023
Impact investing: investment specifically targeting measurable social or environmental outcomes alongside financial returns
Benefit corporations (B Corps): legal corporate form recognized in most states allowing or requiring consideration of stakeholder interests; certified by B Lab
Anti-ESG backlash: political and legal opposition to ESG investing in some US states, arguing it violates fiduciary duties to maximize returns for beneficiaries
lecture_notes.demonstration:
Analyze Unilever under Paul Polman (2009-2019) as a case study in stakeholder capitalism. When Paul Polman became CEO in January 2009, he made an unusual announcement: Unilever would no longer provide quarterly earnings guidance to investors. His rationale: short-term quarterly pressures distorted long-term strategic decisions, and Unilever needed to focus on multi-year sustainability, stakeholder relationships, and durable competitive advantage rather than quarterly targets.

This was a direct rejection of the investor-relations approach that had dominated corporate communication. Polman was betting that long-term investors (who care about sustainability, talent retention, brand reputation, and stakeholder relationships) would support this strategy while short-term traders would exit.

Polman then launched the 'Sustainable Living Plan' committing Unilever to:
- Double revenue while halving environmental impact.
- Source 100% of agricultural raw materials sustainably.
- Help 1 billion people improve their health and wellbeing.
- Achieve net-zero emissions by 2039 (later extended).

These commitments went far beyond normal CSR activities. They were integrated into product development, supply chain management, and strategic planning.

Results:

Financial performance: Unilever's revenue grew from €40 billion to €50 billion during Polman's tenure, with operating margins improving. Total shareholder return was about 150% over Polman's 10 years, roughly matching consumer goods peers. The stakeholder approach did not produce superior returns, but it did not destroy value either.

Stakeholder outcomes:
- Environmental impact reduced substantially in many metrics (water, carbon, waste), though not at the pace initially committed.
- Workforce engagement improved; Unilever became a preferred employer for ESG-focused professionals.
- Customer perceptions improved; brands like Dove and Ben & Jerry's became associated with progressive values.
- Supplier relationships improved through sustainable sourcing programs.
- Community programs in developing markets improved health and water access.

Challenges:
- Operational complexity: sustainability programs added cost and management burden.
- Some initiatives did not achieve stated goals (full supply chain sustainability proved harder than anticipated).
- Activist investor Nelson Peltz joined board in 2022 and pushed for structural changes, arguing Polman's successor (Alan Jope) had allowed Unilever to become unfocused.
- Recent Unilever leadership (Hein Schumacher from 2023) has signaled a more focused approach to sustainability, reducing some commitments while maintaining others.

Lessons and open questions:

1. Stakeholder strategy can work financially. Polman's Unilever did not destroy shareholder value by focusing on stakeholders. But it also did not produce unusual returns. The financial case for stakeholder capitalism is: it can be sustainable, not that it is superior.

2. Tradeoffs are real. Polman made real tradeoffs - rejecting quarterly guidance, committing to expensive sustainability goals, prioritizing long-term capabilities. These tradeoffs may have cost short-term performance.

3. Credibility matters. Polman's consistent messaging and follow-through made his approach credible with employees, customers, and long-term investors. Firms that talk about stakeholders but act on short-term pressures lose credibility with both groups.

4. Activist pressure is real. Peltz's board activism illustrates that stakeholder-focused leaders remain subject to shareholder pressure. Durable stakeholder capitalism requires alignment with investor expectations.

5. The debate continues. Whether Polman's approach will be vindicated or displaced remains uncertain. The post-Polman shift at Unilever suggests that pure stakeholder capitalism is hard to sustain; a hybrid approach may be more durable.

Compare and contrast:

Johnson & Johnson Tylenol crisis (1982): J&J voluntarily recalled 31 million bottles of Tylenol at cost of ~$100M after cyanide tampering incidents. This was a stakeholder-oriented response that protected public safety at shareholder cost. It became a textbook example of how crisis response informed by stakeholder concerns produces long-term benefits.

Purdue Pharma and the opioid crisis: a pharmaceutical company maximized shareholder returns by aggressively marketing OxyContin while downplaying addiction risks. Result: tens of thousands of deaths, massive liability, bankruptcy, criminal charges against executives, reputational destruction of Sackler family. Pure shareholder focus without stakeholder consideration produced catastrophic long-term outcomes.

Boeing 737 MAX: Boeing's shift toward shareholder return emphasis under Jim McNerney and Dennis Muilenburg, while McDonnell Douglas culture displaced engineering culture, contributed to safety culture failures. Result: two fatal crashes, 346 deaths, $20+ billion in direct costs, ongoing reputational damage, FAA oversight. Aggressive shareholder orientation can produce long-term stakeholder and shareholder harm simultaneously.

The pattern: pure shareholder primacy that ignores stakeholder concerns often produces short-term returns but long-term catastrophes. Pure stakeholder capitalism that ignores shareholder returns often becomes unsustainable. The most durable approach considers both, balances them consciously, and makes explicit tradeoffs.

review.value: Shareholder primacy vs. stakeholder capitalism is an ongoing debate, not a settled question. Different jurisdictions, companies, and contexts favor different approaches. Business leaders should understand the arguments on both sides rather than adopting one without reflection

Much of the debate is about time horizons. What appears to be stakeholder-focused action may actually be long-term shareholder value creation. Building employee capability, customer relationships, community trust, and environmental sustainability may pay off over years or decades, making stakeholder focus rational even for shareholder-focused managers

Stakeholder interests can conflict with each other and with shareholders. Higher wages benefit employees but may reduce shareholder returns or raise prices for customers. Environmental protection benefits communities but may raise costs. Any stakeholder-oriented strategy must make explicit choices about how to weigh conflicting interests

A key critique of stakeholder capitalism is that it makes managers accountable to no one - managers can justify any decision by claiming stakeholder considerations. Shareholder primacy, whatever its limits, provides clear accountability. Durable stakeholder approaches need alternative accountability mechanisms

class_schedule.topic: White Collar Crime and Corporate Compliance
learning_objectives: Identify major white collar crimes: fraud, insider trading, money laundering, bribery
Explain the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) and anti-bribery obligations
Analyze effective corporate compliance programs and their legal benefits
Evaluate the use of deferred prosecution agreements and non-prosecution agreements
readings: Mallor et al., Business Law (18th ed.), Ch. 5 - Criminal Law and Business
Miller, Fundamentals of Business Law (11th ed.), Ch. 9
Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (1977)
DOJ Corporate Compliance Program Guidance (2020, updated)
Major FCPA cases: Siemens, Walmart, Goldman Sachs 1MDB

lecture_notes.introduction: White collar crime refers to non-violent crimes committed by business professionals and organizations, typically involving deceit, concealment, or violation of trust. Fraud is the most common category, but other major white collar crimes include insider trading, money laundering, bribery, embezzlement, tax evasion, and anticompetitive conspiracies. These crimes can cause enormous economic damage - the Enron and WorldCom scandals wiped out billions in shareholder value, and the Madoff Ponzi scheme cost investors $65 billion. Unlike traditional crimes, white collar crime typically involves complex schemes over long time periods, sophisticated cover-ups, and evidence in financial records rather than eyewitness accounts. This makes investigation challenging and often requires forensic accounting, cooperating witnesses, and extensive document review. Corporate compliance is the field of preventing, detecting, and responding to white collar crime and regulatory violations within organizations. The Sentencing Guidelines for Organizations (originally 1991, revised multiple times) created significant incentives for effective compliance programs - organizations with effective compliance programs receive substantial reductions in criminal penalties. The DOJ's Corporate Compliance Program Guidance (originally 2017, updated) provides detailed expectations for what constitutes an effective compliance program. This session covers the major white collar crimes, focuses on FCPA (Foreign Corrupt Practices Act) which is particularly important for multinationals, and addresses corporate compliance programs and the enforcement tools available to prosecutors including deferred prosecution agreements.
key_concepts: Fraud: knowing misrepresentation or omission of material fact with intent to induce reliance, causing damages; types include wire fraud, mail fraud, bank fraud, securities fraud, healthcare fraud
Insider trading: trading securities based on material non-public information in breach of fiduciary duty; prosecuted under Rule 10b-5 and the Insider Trading Sanctions Act
Money laundering: converting proceeds of illegal activity into legitimate-appearing assets; Bank Secrecy Act and USA PATRIOT Act impose reporting requirements on financial institutions

Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA, 1977): prohibits US companies and their agents from paying foreign officials to obtain or retain business; anti-bribery provisions and accounting/internal controls provisions
FCPA jurisdiction: broad extraterritorial reach - covers US companies, foreign companies with US securities listings, any conduct with a US nexus (email, financial transactions)
FCPA penalties: criminal fines up to $2M for companies and $250K per individual violation, plus up to 5 years imprisonment; civil penalties from SEC; disgorgement of profits
Effective compliance program elements: policies, training, risk assessment, reporting mechanisms, investigation procedures, discipline, continuous improvement
Deferred Prosecution Agreement (DPA): agreement where prosecution defers charges in exchange for cooperation and compliance improvements; common in major corporate criminal cases
Non-Prosecution Agreement (NPA): similar to DPA but no charges filed; cooperation and compliance changes replace prosecution
Monitorship: court-appointed or DOJ-approved monitor oversees corporate compliance as condition of settlement; typical for major FCPA and other corporate criminal cases
lecture_notes.demonstration:
Analyze the Walmart FCPA case (2012-2019). Facts: Walmart expanded aggressively into Mexico in the 1990s and 2000s. Internal investigations revealed that Walmart de Mexico had been paying bribes to Mexican officials to obtain permits, zoning approvals, and other regulatory favors. Payments reportedly totaled $24 million. When Walmart HQ learned of the bribes, the internal investigation was allegedly suppressed to avoid damaging Walmart's growth story. The New York Times published an expose in April 2012, triggering DOJ and SEC investigations.

Legal analysis:

1. FCPA anti-bribery violations: Walmart's Mexico subsidiary paid bribes to government officials to obtain benefits for the business. This violated the FCPA's anti-bribery provisions. Walmart's parent company had knowledge and was responsible for subsidiary actions.

2. FCPA accounting violations: Walmart's books and records did not accurately reflect the bribe payments, which were disguised as legitimate expenses. This violated the FCPA's accounting provisions that require accurate books and records.

3. Internal controls: Walmart's internal controls did not prevent or detect the improper payments for years. This violated the FCPA's internal controls provisions.

4. Similar conduct in other countries: Investigation revealed similar issues in India, Brazil, and China. Walmart was implicated in FCPA violations across multiple jurisdictions.

Results (2019 settlement):
- Walmart paid $282 million in total settlements: $138 million criminal fine to DOJ, $144 million civil penalty to SEC for accounting violations.
- Walmart subsidiary Walmart Brazil pleaded guilty to violating FCPA accounting provisions.
- Walmart entered into a three-year non-prosecution agreement.
- No charges against individual Walmart executives (unusual for such a large case).
- Walmart agreed to continued compliance monitoring and reforms.

Broader implications:

1. FCPA is actively enforced. The DOJ and SEC pursue FCPA cases aggressively. Penalties can be substantial. Walmart's $282 million settlement was large but not unprecedented - Siemens paid $1.6 billion in 2008, and Goldman Sachs paid $2.9 billion for 1MDB-related violations in 2020.

2. Global operations require global compliance. Multinational firms cannot assume that operations in countries where bribery is common will not create FCPA exposure. US-based firms are subject to FCPA wherever they operate.

3. Effective compliance matters. Walmart's inadequate compliance program was a significant factor. Firms with effective compliance receive mitigation credit under sentencing guidelines. Firms without effective compliance face harsher penalties.

4. Cooperation and remediation affect outcomes. Walmart's cooperation in the investigation and its subsequent compliance improvements likely reduced penalties. Failing to cooperate or cover-up efforts would have made things worse.

5. Internal investigations must be taken seriously. When the Walmart internal investigation revealed bribes, it should have been fully investigated and reported rather than suppressed. Suppression created additional exposure and turned a manageable compliance failure into a major scandal.

Practical compliance recommendations:

1. Written policies: FCPA and anti-bribery policies prohibiting improper payments, with clear definitions of prohibited conduct.

2. Training: regular training for employees at all levels, particularly those interacting with foreign officials. Tailored training for high-risk roles and regions.

3. Risk assessment: identify high-risk operations (particular countries, agents, transaction types) and implement enhanced controls.

4. Third-party due diligence: vet agents, distributors, joint venture partners for corruption risk. Many FCPA cases involve third-party conduct.

5. Internal controls: expense controls, approval requirements, audit trails. Prevent undocumented cash payments.

6. Reporting mechanisms: hotlines, anonymous reporting, protected whistleblower channels.

7. Investigation capabilities: trained investigators, forensic accounting, legal support. Don't suppress findings.

8. Discipline: consistent consequences for violations, including termination when warranted.

9. Continuous improvement: periodic review and updating of compliance program based on risk changes and lessons learned.

Compare: Siemens FCPA case (2008) - $1.6 billion settlement, involved systematic bribery in Germany, Mexico, Venezuela, and elsewhere. Odebrecht (2016) - $3.5 billion settlement for bribery in multiple Latin American countries. Goldman Sachs 1MDB (2020) - $2.9 billion for fraud in Malaysia investment fund. Each illustrates that FCPA and anti-bribery enforcement is active and expensive for noncompliant firms.

review.value: Major fraud, insider trading, FCPA, and money laundering cases are actively prosecuted. Executives convicted of serious white collar crimes often serve long prison sentences. The enforcement is real, not theoretical
The FCPA applies to US companies worldwide and to many non-US companies with US connections. Multinational operations require FCPA compliance regardless of local practices. Ignorance is not a defense

US Sentencing Guidelines and DOJ policy provide significant benefits for organizations with effective compliance programs. Implementing real compliance (not just paper programs) reduces criminal exposure and improves outcomes when problems occur

When compliance violations are discovered, cover-up efforts turn manageable problems into major scandals. Firms should investigate findings thoroughly, report when required, and remediate actively. Walmart's attempted suppression of the Mexico investigation illustrates how not to handle compliance issues

class_schedule.topic: Privacy, Data Protection, and Cybersecurity Law
learning_objectives: Identify the major privacy and data protection laws: GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, GLBA
Analyze cybersecurity obligations and breach notification requirements
Evaluate international privacy law compliance for multinationals
Develop a framework for privacy-by-design in business operations
readings: Mallor et al., Business Law (18th ed.), Ch. 50 - Internet Law and Privacy
Miller, Fundamentals of Business Law (11th ed.), Ch. 35
GDPR (EU General Data Protection Regulation), California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)
Major data breach cases: Equifax, Target, Facebook, SolarWinds

lecture_notes.introduction: Privacy and data protection have become central legal concerns in the digital age. The collection, use, storage, and sharing of personal data is now regulated by a complex patchwork of federal sector-specific laws (HIPAA for health, GLBA for financial, COPPA for children), state laws (California CCPA and others), and foreign laws with extraterritorial reach (EU GDPR). For businesses, privacy law compliance is both a legal obligation and a brand and trust issue - mishandled personal data can result in fines, litigation, reputational damage, and loss of customer trust. The 2018 EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) has become the most influential privacy law globally, partly because of its broad extraterritorial reach (applies to any company processing data of EU residents regardless of where the company is based) and partly because of its substantial penalties (up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million, whichever is higher). California's CCPA (effective 2020) was the first comprehensive US state privacy law, followed by Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut, Utah, and others. Federal privacy legislation has been proposed multiple times but has not passed. The US lacks a comprehensive federal privacy law, which creates compliance complexity for multi-state businesses. Data breach notification laws in all 50 states require businesses to notify affected individuals when their personal information is compromised. These laws have been enforced through lawsuits, state attorney general actions, and FTC enforcement. Major breaches at Equifax (2017, 147 million Americans' data), Target (2013, 40 million credit cards), and others have led to hundreds of millions in penalties and private settlements. Cybersecurity has emerged as a specific concern beyond general data protection. NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) provides cybersecurity frameworks. Specific sectors have cybersecurity requirements (SOX for public companies, HIPAA for healthcare, GLBA for financial services, NERC CIP for electric utilities). This session covers the major privacy and data protection laws and addresses compliance frameworks.
key_concepts: Personal data/Personally Identifiable Information (PII): information that identifies or could identify a specific individual, directly or indirectly; definitions vary by law
Data controller: entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data; has primary compliance obligations under most privacy laws
Data processor: entity that processes personal data on behalf of a controller; must follow controller's instructions and has its own compliance obligations

GDPR (2018): comprehensive EU privacy law applying to any entity processing EU resident data; requires legal basis for processing, data subject rights, privacy by design, breach notification within 72 hours, Data Protection Officer for certain organizations; penalties up to 4% of global revenue
CCPA/CPRA (California): comprehensive state privacy law giving California consumers rights to know what data is collected, request deletion, opt out of sale, non-discrimination
HIPAA: US health privacy law applying to healthcare providers, health plans, and clearinghouses; covered entities must protect PHI (Protected Health Information)
GLBA (Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act): US financial privacy law requiring financial institutions to explain information-sharing practices and protect customer data
COPPA (Children's Online Privacy Protection Act): US law protecting children under 13 from online collection of personal information without verifiable parental consent
Data breach notification: requirements to notify affected individuals and regulators when personal data is compromised; all 50 US states have breach notification laws with varying requirements
Privacy by design: approach to privacy compliance that incorporates privacy considerations into system design from the start rather than adding them after development
lecture_notes.demonstration:
Analyze the Equifax 2017 data breach. Facts: Equifax is one of three major US credit reporting agencies, holding sensitive financial and personal data on over 200 million Americans. In 2017, Equifax disclosed that a data breach had exposed personal information of 147 million Americans, including Social Security numbers, birthdates, addresses, credit card numbers, and in some cases driver's license numbers. The breach was enabled by Equifax's failure to patch a known vulnerability in Apache Struts web framework - the patch had been available for over two months before the breach.

Legal implications:

1. Federal enforcement: FTC pursued Equifax for deceptive and unfair practices. The FTC, CFPB, and all 50 state attorneys general reached a combined settlement in 2019 for $575-700 million.

2. SEC enforcement: Equifax settled with SEC for $23 million for disclosure failures. Some Equifax executives sold stock in the period between the breach discovery and its public disclosure - potential insider trading violations. CIO Jun Ying was criminally charged and pleaded guilty to insider trading.

3. State enforcement: Massachusetts, California, Washington, and other states pursued additional state-law claims. New York imposed specific cybersecurity requirements for financial services companies (23 NYCRR 500, 2017).

4. Private litigation: Consumer class actions resulted in settlements providing individual compensation (cash payments, free credit monitoring) to affected consumers. Total class action settlement value was approximately $1.4 billion.

5. Congressional scrutiny: Multiple congressional hearings, investigations, and proposed legislation.

Total cost to Equifax: approximately $2 billion in direct costs, including penalties, settlements, legal fees, breach response, and remediation investments.

Business implications and lessons:

1. Cybersecurity is a board-level issue. Equifax's board should have been monitoring cybersecurity risk more closely. The failure to patch a known vulnerability was an operational failure that escalated to a board-level crisis.

2. Data minimization reduces risk. Equifax held enormous amounts of personal data that it could not protect adequately. Collecting only necessary data reduces the attack surface.

3. Vendor management matters. Third-party software vulnerabilities create risk. Firms must monitor their software supply chain and respond quickly to security advisories.

4. Breach response must be planned and practiced. Equifax's breach response was widely criticized - delayed public disclosure, confusing consumer information, overwhelmed response systems. A pre-planned breach response with drills would have reduced the damage.

5. Executive accountability: The CEO (Richard Smith) was forced to resign, and several executives were fired or retired. Individual accountability in cybersecurity incidents has become more common.

6. Insurance and liability: Cybersecurity insurance has become a major market, with firms paying millions in annual premiums. Insurance does not eliminate liability but transfers some of it.

7. Regulatory trajectory: Breach experience has led to stronger regulation, including the NY DFS cybersecurity rule, CCPA, and (eventually) likely federal privacy legislation.

Compare: Target 2013 (40 million credit cards, $200+ million in settlements); Yahoo 2013-2014 (3 billion accounts, initially concealed during Verizon acquisition); Marriott 2018 (383 million guests affected); Facebook Cambridge Analytica (87 million users affected, FTC $5 billion settlement 2019); SolarWinds 2020 (software supply chain attack affecting many government and business customers).

Each illustrates different dimensions of data security and privacy law compliance. The common themes: (1) serious breaches are likely given the value of data to attackers and the complexity of IT systems; (2) breach costs are substantial and rising; (3) regulatory enforcement is intensifying; (4) board oversight and executive accountability are increasing.

review.value: Privacy and data protection are regulated in most major jurisdictions, often with extraterritorial effect. GDPR applies to EU residents' data regardless of where the processing occurs. Multiple states have comprehensive privacy laws. Compliance across jurisdictions is complex and ongoing

All 50 US states have breach notification laws, as do most countries with comprehensive privacy laws. Failing to notify promptly can result in additional penalties. Breach response must be planned, practiced, and ready to execute quickly

Cybersecurity incidents can cost hundreds of millions to billions of dollars. They are now among the top business risks. Board-level attention, executive accountability, and significant investment in security are essential for any organization handling significant personal data

Building privacy considerations into systems from the start is cheaper and more effective than adding them later. Firms that treat privacy as a development constraint rather than an afterthought build better systems with less legal risk

class_schedule.topic: Integration: Legal Risk Management and Ethical Leadership
learning_objectives: Integrate legal and ethical analysis into business decision-making
Develop a personal framework for handling ethical dilemmas
Analyze the role of legal risk management in strategic decisions
Prepare for case-based analysis of complex legal and ethical issues
readings: Mallor et al., Business Law (18th ed.), Ch. 4 review on ethics and CSR
Trevino & Nelson, Managing Business Ethics (8th ed.), Ch. 1-2
Bazerman & Tenbrunsel, Blind Spots: Why We Fail to Do What's Right and What to Do About It (2011)
Reflection questions: how will you decide?

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session synthesizes the legal and ethical concepts from the course and addresses how to integrate them into real business decisions. Legal compliance and ethical behavior are related but distinct. Many actions are both legal and ethical (the easy cases). Some actions are illegal and unethical (also easy, to be avoided). The hard cases are those where legal and ethical considerations diverge or where the right answer is unclear even within one framework. This course has provided tools for both types of analysis: legal frameworks (sources of law, specific statutes and doctrines, court decisions) and ethical frameworks (utilitarian, deontological, virtue ethics, justice theory, stakeholder theory). But frameworks are not enough. Good legal and ethical decision-making also requires character - the willingness to do what is right even when it is costly or unpopular, and the humility to recognize when you might be wrong. Mary Gentile's 'Giving Voice to Values' approach argues that most people know what is right but struggle to act on it; the key skill is developing the confidence and strategies to speak up against unethical practices. Bazerman and Tenbrunsel's research on 'Blind Spots' shows that we often fail ethical tests without realizing it - through bounded ethicality, motivated reasoning, and convenient ignorance. Awareness of these cognitive limits is essential for ethical leadership. This session also addresses legal risk management as a strategic discipline. Most strategic decisions have legal implications, and understanding those implications before acting is cheaper than discovering them in litigation. Lawyers are essential, but lawyers alone cannot replace management judgment about whether to accept legal risks or pursue alternatives. The goal is not to eliminate all legal risk - that is impossible and would prevent any business activity - but to make informed decisions about which risks to accept and how to manage them. Finally, we'll discuss leadership responsibility. Executives who lead ethical organizations are not just personally ethical; they create systems (culture, incentives, reporting mechanisms, discipline) that make ethical behavior the default and unethical behavior difficult. Bad ethics is often a leadership failure, not just individual misbehavior.
key_concepts: Integration of legal and ethical analysis: applying both legal compliance frameworks and ethical reasoning to business decisions, with awareness of where they diverge
Legal risk management: identifying, assessing, and mitigating legal risks in strategic and operational decisions; involves lawyers but requires management judgment
Ethical blind spots (Bazerman & Tenbrunsel): systematic ways people fail ethical tests without realizing it; bounded ethicality, motivated reasoning, role morality, ethical fading
Bounded ethicality: psychological limits on people's capacity to recognize and respond to ethical issues; we often fail ethical tests not from bad intentions but from cognitive blind spots

Giving Voice to Values (Mary Gentile): approach to ethics training that focuses on how to act on values you already hold rather than on what the right values are; emphasizes practical strategies for speaking up
Tone at the top: the ethical messaging and behavior of senior leadership; often the most important factor in organizational ethics culture
Speak-up culture: organizational conditions that encourage employees to raise concerns about unethical or illegal conduct without fear of retaliation
Whistleblower protection: legal protections for employees who report violations of law to authorities; Sarbanes-Oxley, Dodd-Frank, state whistleblower laws
Ethical decision-making process: (1) recognize the ethical issue, (2) get the facts, (3) evaluate alternatives using ethical frameworks, (4) make and test a decision, (5) act and reflect
Moral courage: the willingness to do what is right despite personal cost, social pressure, or inconvenience; distinct from intellectual knowledge of what is right
lecture_notes.demonstration:
Work through an integrated legal and ethical case: You are the CFO of a technology startup. You discover that the CEO has been including non-existent customers in revenue projections to investors. The projections have been used to raise new investment capital. When you ask the CEO, she says 'Everyone in startup land does this, and if we disclose it our stock will crash and we'll all lose our jobs.' What should you do?

Legal analysis:

1. Securities fraud: Misrepresenting revenue to investors is securities fraud under Rule 10b-5. Investors relied on the projections in making investment decisions. The CEO's statements are material and intentionally false. This is criminal fraud with potential prison sentences.

2. Your liability: As CFO, you are likely deemed to have knowledge of the fraud. If you participate (by remaining silent and signing financial statements) you could be personally charged. Executives who 'look the other way' have been convicted of securities fraud. Ignorance of specific details does not protect you if you had reason to know.

3. Sarbanes-Oxley Section 302: If the company is publicly traded, the CEO and CFO must certify the accuracy of financial reports. Knowingly false certifications are criminal.

4. Audit committee disclosure: If you are public, you must disclose this to the audit committee. Failure to escalate compliance issues is itself a violation.

5. Investor protection: Investors who buy based on false information can sue, and individual executives can be personally liable for damages, disgorgement of ill-gotten gains, and SEC bars from serving as officers/directors.

6. Whistleblower laws: SOX, Dodd-Frank, and state whistleblower statutes protect you if you report the fraud. You could be entitled to a bounty (up to 30% of recovery) under Dodd-Frank. Retaliation against you would be an additional violation.

Ethical analysis:

1. Utilitarian: does the harm from the fraud exceed the harm from disclosing it? Probably yes - investors who bought based on false information have been materially harmed; the firm's employees may face job loss either way, but the aggregated harm from ongoing fraud outweighs the harm from correction. Disclosure and correction produces net better outcomes.

2. Deontological: using investors as means to raise capital through deception violates their dignity as autonomous decision-makers. This is clearly wrong by Kantian analysis. The duty to tell the truth is strong.

3. Virtue ethics: what would a person of good character do? A virtuous person would not participate in fraud and would report it when discovered. Integrity, honesty, and courage are virtues.

4. Justice: behind a veil of ignorance, would we accept rules that let companies defraud investors? No. Fair treatment of investors is fundamental to investment markets.

5. Stakeholder: consider effects on shareholders, employees, customers, the CEO, you. Disclosure protects shareholders (primary victims), hurts the CEO (justified consequence of her actions), may harm employees short-term but enables sustainable business long-term. You face personal cost but preserve your integrity and avoid criminal liability.

Decision: You must disclose and correct the fraud. The legal analysis leaves no room for continuation (criminal liability for participation). The ethical analysis converges on the same conclusion across all major frameworks.

How to act:

1. Document what you know. Write down the specific conversations, dates, and evidence.

2. Consult independent counsel (not the company's regular law firm, which may have conflicts). Counsel who specializes in securities fraud and whistleblowing can advise on the legal and procedural steps.

3. Report to the audit committee or (if no effective audit committee) directly to the board. Sarbanes-Oxley provides specific procedures for escalating such issues.

4. If internal reporting is suppressed, report to the SEC under their whistleblower program. Retain records of your reporting and the company's response.

5. Consult with labor counsel about protecting yourself from retaliation. SOX whistleblower protection is substantial but complex.

6. Prepare for personal consequences: possible termination (which SOX protects against, but litigation may be required), professional reputation, and career impact. Ethical leadership sometimes comes at personal cost.

Real parallel cases:

- Sherron Watkins at Enron wrote a memo to CEO Ken Lay warning about accounting irregularities. She went public after the company began collapsing and testified before Congress. She faced retaliation but became known as a principled whistleblower.

- Erica Williams at Wells Fargo raised concerns about fake accounts. She was retaliated against and eventually left the company. Her case became part of the broader fraud case against Wells Fargo that resulted in $3 billion in settlements.

- Cynthia Cooper at WorldCom was the internal auditor who discovered the $3.8 billion accounting fraud. Her role in escalating the fraud led to the company's collapse and criminal charges against executives.

What if you had stayed silent?

If the fraud continued and was eventually discovered (which is likely - securities fraud tends to unwind when business conditions change or someone else reports), you would face criminal liability. Participants in Enron, WorldCom, Theranos, and similar cases have served long prison sentences. Your defense would be difficult because as CFO you should have known. The only path to avoiding personal liability is to not participate.

Synthesis: Some cases are hard because legal and ethical analyses diverge. Many cases are easier than they feel because both analyses converge - they just require courage to act. Often, the hardest part is not knowing what is right but doing it when it's costly. Develop the habit of seeing ethical issues clearly, analyzing them rigorously, and acting with integrity. Over a career, this will serve you far better than convenient silence.

review.key: Legal and Ethical Analysis Both Required
review.value: Business decisions require both legal and ethical analysis. Legal compliance alone is insufficient for good decision-making. Ethical analysis alone is insufficient without understanding legal constraints. Integrate both perspectives

Knowing what is right is often easier than acting on it. Moral courage - the willingness to do what is right despite cost - is a skill that can be developed through practice, preparation, and supportive structures. Do not wait for a crisis to decide how you will act

Organizations get ethical behavior through systems (culture, incentives, reporting, discipline) more than through individual good intentions. Leaders who build good systems produce ethical organizations; leaders who rely on individual virtue without system support produce vulnerabilities

Ethical behavior compounds over time. Small ethical choices build habits of mind and reputation that produce better outcomes over a career. Small unethical compromises compound in the opposite direction. Invest in integrity as you would invest in any long-term asset

quizzes.topic: Legal System, Ethics, and Contract Formation

questions.question: Compare utilitarian, deontological, and virtue ethics approaches to a specific business dilemma: a pharmaceutical company considering whether to price a new life-saving drug at $500,000 per year per patient vs. $50,000 per year. Analyze the decision using all three frameworks.
questions.answer:
This pricing decision is a paradigmatic ethical dilemma that produces different recommendations under different frameworks. Each framework offers distinct insight.

UTILITARIAN ANALYSIS (Mill, Bentham):

Utilitarianism evaluates actions by their consequences, specifically the aggregate welfare or happiness produced. At each price, consider all affected parties and estimate the net welfare effect.

At $500,000/year: Total revenue per 10,000 patients = $5 billion annually. Assume 60% of patients (6,000) can be treated (through insurance and subsidies); 40% (4,000) go without treatment. Company profit enables significant R&D for future treatments. Untreated patients suffer severe illness or death. Insurance premiums rise, affecting other patients. Aggregated welfare: high corporate returns balanced against major patient suffering. The math depends on how we weigh corporate returns vs. patient health. If we value life highly (which utilitarians do, in the sense of weighing quality-adjusted life years), the net welfare is likely negative compared to lower pricing.

At $50,000/year: Total revenue = $500 million. Assume 95% of patients (9,500) can be treated; 5% (500) still go without. Company profit is much lower but positive; R&D funding may be reduced. Fewer untreated patients, more lives saved, less suffering. Insurance premiums rise less dramatically. Aggregated welfare: lower corporate returns balanced against much better patient outcomes. Likely higher net welfare than $500,000.

Utilitarian recommendation: the $50,000 price appears better under utilitarian analysis because it produces more net welfare. Pure utilitarianism would recommend even lower pricing if that treats more patients without reducing aggregate welfare through reduced R&D. Utilitarianism would accept company profit but not extreme rent extraction.

However, a thoughtful utilitarian also considers future lives: if high prices fund more R&D for additional drugs, the expected future welfare from those drugs enters the calculation. This is why rule utilitarianism (rather than act utilitarianism) sometimes justifies patent protection - the rules that incentivize innovation produce more welfare over time than rules that don't.

DEONTOLOGICAL ANALYSIS (Kant):

Kantian ethics evaluates actions by whether they conform to moral principles derived from reason, regardless of consequences. The categorical imperative has two relevant formulations:

1. Universal Law: Could we will that our pricing rule become universal? If every pharmaceutical company priced life-saving drugs beyond what most patients could afford, some patients would always die for lack of treatment. Could we rationally will this as a universal principle? Probably not - we would not want our own family members to die for lack of affordable treatment. The universal law test seems to fail.

2. Humanity as End: Do we treat patients as ends in themselves or merely as means? Pricing that knowingly excludes some patients from life-saving treatment treats them as instruments for extracting profit rather than as persons of inherent dignity. Kant is clear that persons must not be used merely as means.

Kantian recommendation: Pricing should ensure access to treatment for all who need it. The exact price could vary based on sustainable business operations, but the principle is that deliberate exclusion of patients is morally impermissible. $50,000 is better than $500,000 but the Kantian might argue for even lower pricing consistent with business sustainability.

VIRTUE ETHICS ANALYSIS (Aristotle):

Virtue ethics asks: what would a person or organization of good character do? Virtues relevant to this decision include:

- Justice: treating patients fairly and equitably; not exploiting their desperate need for treatment.
- Compassion: concerning oneself with the suffering of others.
- Responsibility: fulfilling the role of pharmaceutical company as serving patient health, not just extracting profit.
- Courage: willing to price moderately even at cost to short-term profits.
- Prudence: wisdom to balance multiple considerations.

A virtuous pharmaceutical company would not adopt pricing that treats desperately ill patients as opportunities for maximum profit extraction. Such behavior reflects vices: greed, indifference, injustice.

Virtue ethics recommendation: moderate pricing that ensures broad access while maintaining company sustainability. A virtuous company might charge a significant premium to support R&D and profits, but would not deliberately exclude patients. The exact price depends on circumstances, but the $500,000 price reflects vices incompatible with a virtuous organization.

The Shkreli case (2015) where Martin Shkreli raised Daraprim from $13.50 to $750 per pill is universally condemned under virtue ethics because it reflects greed and exploitation at the expense of vulnerable patients. Shkreli himself seemed indifferent to public condemnation, which itself reflects character failure.

SYNTHESIS:

All three frameworks converge on the view that the $500,000 price is ethically problematic and the $50,000 price is substantially better. The frameworks disagree about some details:

- Utilitarianism allows for some profit to fund future R&D.
- Deontology focuses on not treating patients merely as means.
- Virtue ethics considers character and social responsibility.

But no framework supports pricing that deliberately excludes large numbers of patients from life-saving treatment for maximum profit extraction.

PRACTICAL CONSIDERATIONS:

In practice, pharmaceutical pricing involves many factors beyond pure ethics:
- Development costs ($1-3 billion per approved drug on average).
- Clinical trial costs and failures.
- Patent life (20 years from filing, typically 10-12 years of effective exclusivity after approval).
- Competition from generic drugs after patent expiration.
- Orphan drug incentives for rare diseases.
- Health insurance markets and government negotiation.
- Global pricing dynamics.

A thoughtful ethical analysis recognizes these constraints while still insisting on some floor below which pricing becomes ethically indefensible. The exact floor is debatable; that there is a floor is not.

Contemporary examples:

- Humira (adalimumab) for rheumatoid arthritis: $80,000/year, aggressive pricing defended on R&D grounds but criticized for extreme rent extraction. $220 billion in total sales over its patent life. Now facing biosimilar competition.

- Sovaldi for hepatitis C: $84,000 for 12-week course, criticized as overpriced but actually provides cure for a disease that otherwise leads to liver failure and death. Over time, coverage expanded and prices declined.

- Spinraza for spinal muscular atrophy: $750,000 first year, $375,000 per year ongoing. Very small patient population. Price is defended as necessary to justify rare disease R&D.

These examples show that pharmaceutical pricing is genuinely difficult - high prices can be justified by innovation costs and rare disease economics, but extreme prices that exclude patients from treatment cross an ethical line that all major frameworks recognize.

rubric.key: Clear application of all three frameworks with different reasoning
Recognition of practical considerations and tradeoffs

questions.question: A verbal agreement is made between a US company and a Mexican supplier for delivery of 10,000 units of electronic components at $50 per unit, delivery in 90 days. (a) Is this a valid contract? (b) Does the Statute of Frauds apply? (c) What law governs (US common law or UCC)? (d) What remedies are available if the supplier fails to deliver?
questions.answer:
This question tests multiple contract law concepts including formation, Statute of Frauds, UCC vs common law, and remedies.

(a) Validity of the contract:

A valid contract requires: (1) offer, (2) acceptance, (3) consideration, (4) capacity, (5) legality. The verbal agreement appears to contain:
- Offer: The supplier proposes selling 10,000 units at $50 each, delivery in 90 days.
- Acceptance: The buyer agrees.
- Consideration: Buyer promises to pay $500,000 total; supplier promises to deliver goods.
- Capacity: Assumed (both are business entities with capacity).
- Legality: Electronics sales are legal.

The elements of contract formation appear satisfied in principle.

However, the contract may not be enforceable due to the Statute of Frauds (see b).

(b) Statute of Frauds analysis:

The Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable. For sale of goods, UCC Section 2-201 requires a writing for contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more.

This contract is for electronic components (goods, not services) worth $500,000 - far more than $500. Therefore, the Statute of Frauds applies and generally requires a writing.

The writing must: (1) indicate that a contract for sale has been made, (2) be signed by the party to be charged (the party against whom enforcement is sought), (3) specify the quantity. The writing does not need to contain all the contract terms.

Exceptions to the UCC Statute of Frauds:
- Merchant's confirmation rule (UCC 2-201(2)): between merchants, a written confirmation that is not objected to within 10 days of receipt is enforceable against the receiving party even without their signature.
- Specially manufactured goods (UCC 2-201(3)(a)): if the goods are custom-made and not suitable for sale to others, the contract is enforceable even without a writing.
- Admission (UCC 2-201(3)(b)): if a party admits in court proceedings that a contract was made, the contract is enforceable up to the quantity admitted.
- Part performance (UCC 2-201(3)(c)): if goods have been paid for or received and accepted, the contract is enforceable as to the goods paid for or accepted.

Without a writing and without an exception applying, the contract is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. The oral agreement would need to be reduced to writing (an email, a purchase order, a letter confirmation) for enforcement.

(c) Governing law:

This is a sale of goods contract, so UCC Article 2 applies (adopted by all 50 states with minor variations). UCC governs formation, performance, breach, and remedies for sales of goods, replacing common law for such transactions.

But wait - this is a cross-border transaction between a US buyer and a Mexican supplier. Additional considerations:

1. Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG, 1980): The CISG applies to contracts for sale of goods between parties whose places of business are in different contracting states (Mexico and the US are both signatories). CISG would automatically apply UNLESS the parties opt out.

2. Choice of law clause: In a written contract, the parties can specify which law governs (e.g., 'This contract shall be governed by the laws of the State of California, excluding the CISG'). Without such a clause, the CISG applies by default for international commercial sales between CISG parties.

3. CISG differences from UCC: The CISG does not have a Statute of Frauds requirement (Article 11 - contract may be proved by any means, including witnesses). This means that under CISG, an oral contract for international sale of goods CAN be enforced without a writing.

So the analysis depends on whether CISG applies:
- If CISG applies (default for US-Mexico goods contract), the oral contract is enforceable in principle.
- If CISG is opted out and UCC applies, the $500 Statute of Frauds requires a writing.

In practice, sophisticated commercial contracts typically include choice of law provisions. Without a choice, the CISG default provides more enforceability for oral contracts than UCC would.

(d) Remedies for non-delivery:

Assuming the contract is enforceable (with writing or under CISG), the buyer has several remedies for the supplier's failure to deliver:

1. Cover and damages (UCC 2-712 or CISG Article 75): The buyer can purchase substitute goods from another supplier and recover the difference between the cover price and the contract price as damages. Example: if the buyer pays $55/unit from another supplier, the buyer can recover $5 x 10,000 = $50,000 in damages (plus incidental and consequential damages).

2. Market price damages (UCC 2-713 or CISG Article 76): If the buyer doesn't cover, damages are the difference between market price and contract price at the time the buyer learned of the breach. Example: if market price is $52/unit at the time of breach, damages are $2 x 10,000 = $20,000.

3. Specific performance (UCC 2-716 or CISG Article 46): In some cases, the buyer can seek specific performance requiring the supplier to deliver the goods. This is more common for unique or rare goods, but not for ordinary electronic components.

4. Consequential damages (UCC 2-715): Additional damages for losses foreseeable at contracting that result from the breach. Example: if the buyer lost $200,000 in production because it could not get the components for 3 weeks, that loss may be recoverable.

5. Incidental damages: Costs of finding substitute goods, increased shipping, inspection, etc.

6. Breach of warranty: If delivered goods do not conform (partial delivery or defective goods), warranty remedies apply.

Practical enforcement challenges:

- Cross-border enforcement: Even with a judgment in the US, enforcing it against Mexican supplier assets requires recognition and enforcement in Mexico, which varies.
- Arbitration: Many international commercial contracts specify arbitration (ICC, AAA, etc.) rather than court litigation. Arbitration awards are generally more enforceable internationally through the New York Convention.
- Costs: Cross-border litigation is expensive and time-consuming. Often parties settle rather than litigate.
- Business relationship: Sophisticated buyers often prefer to maintain supplier relationships rather than pursue litigation, even when legally available.

Recommended practice: Always reduce commercial agreements to writing, even if the Statute of Frauds or CISG does not require it. Include governing law, dispute resolution (arbitration clause), and key commercial terms. Oral contracts create disputes about exactly what was agreed, not just whether a contract exists. The cost of a good contract is much lower than the cost of litigation.

rubric.key: Correct analysis of contract formation elements
Statute of Frauds analysis with UCC $500 threshold
Correct identification of CISG vs UCC for international sale

questions.question: Apply the McDonnell Douglas burden-shifting framework to an age discrimination case: a 55-year-old employee is laid off during a reduction in force, while all 10 retained employees in his department are under 40. His previous performance reviews were all 'exceeds expectations.' Walk through the framework step by step.
questions.answer:
The McDonnell Douglas burden-shifting framework is the standard method for analyzing indirect evidence of employment discrimination under Title VII, ADEA, and similar statutes. It was established by the Supreme Court in McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green (1973) and remains the dominant framework for disparate treatment cases.

The framework has three stages:

1. Plaintiff's prima facie case.
2. Defendant's legitimate non-discriminatory reason.
